content:
# Deuteronomy 21 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 21 (23 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents compared:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

## v.1 — Word order and subordinate clause placement

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "If in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess, a slain person is found fallen in the open field" |
| LOGOS | "If a slain person is found in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess, lying in the open field" |
| PNEUMA | "If a person is found slain, lying in a field in the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess" |

**Ruling:** LOGOS's word order is most natural in English. EMET's fronting of the locative phrase is awkward. PNEUMA drops "the open" before "field." Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.2 — "distance" (singular, LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "[the distance]" bracketed (EMET)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "they shall measure [the distance] to the cities" |
| LOGOS | "measure the distances to the cities" |
| PNEUMA | "measure the distance from the body to the surrounding towns" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *middadta* = measure. The object (distance) is implied. LOGOS: "distances" (plural). PNEUMA adds "from the body" and "surrounding." Consensus follows LOGOS without the bracket.

---

## v.4 — "wadi with running water" (EMET) vs. "valley with running water" (LOGOS) vs. "valley where there is a stream that never runs dry" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "a wadi with running water" |
| LOGOS | "a valley with running water" |
| PNEUMA | "a valley where there is a stream that never runs dry" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *nachal eitan* = "perennial wadi/stream-valley." EMET: "wadi" is technically precise but less accessible. LOGOS: "valley with running water" is accessible and retains the water-flow element. PNEUMA expands *eitan* into "never runs dry" — interpretive expansion. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.5 — "sons of Levi" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "Levitical priests" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the priests, the sons of Levi" |
| LOGOS | "the priests, the sons of Levi" |
| PNEUMA | "The Levitical priests" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ha-kohanim bney Levi* = "the priests, the sons of Levi." EMET/LOGOS preserve the full designation. PNEUMA condenses. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.5 — "minister to him" (EMET) vs. "serve him" (LOGOS) vs. "minister before him" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to minister to him" |
| LOGOS | "to serve him" |
| PNEUMA | "to minister before him" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *le-sharet oto* = "to minister to/serve him." EMET/PNEUMA: "minister." LOGOS: "serve." Consensus follows EMET ("minister to him") — "minister" is the standard liturgical term for priestly service.

---

## v.5 — "settled" (LOGOS) vs. "decided" (EMET) vs. resolved by their decision" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "every dispute and every assault shall be decided" |
| LOGOS | "every dispute and every assault shall be settled" |
| PNEUMA | "every legal dispute and act of violence is settled by their decision" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *yihyeh* = "shall be." PNEUMA adds "by their decision" (redundant) and "legal." EMET: "decided." LOGOS: "settled." Consensus follows LOGOS ("settled").

---

## v.8 — Imperative form of atonement prayer

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Atone, O LORD, for your people Yisra'el" |
| LOGOS | "Accept atonement, O LORD, for your people Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "Accept this atonement for your people Israel, whom you redeemed, O LORD" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *kapper YHWH le-amkha* = "Atone, O LORD, for your people." EMET is the bare imperative. LOGOS: "Accept atonement" is a natural English rendering of the Piel imperative. PNEUMA adds "this" and moves "O LORD" to the end. Consensus follows LOGOS (clearest liturgical form without addition).

---

## v.9 — "when you do what is right" (LOGOS) vs. "for you do what is right" (EMET)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "for you do what is right in the eyes of the LORD" |
| LOGOS | "when you do what is right in the sight of the LORD" |
| PNEUMA | "since you have done what is right in the LORD's sight" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ki ta'aseh ha-yashar be-eyney YHWH* = "when/for you do the right in the eyes of the LORD." EMET: "for." LOGOS: "when." PNEUMA: "since you have done" (past tense). Consensus follows LOGOS ("when") as the conditional that links the purgation to the action.

---

## v.11 — "a woman of beautiful form" (EMET) vs. "a beautiful woman" (LOGOS) vs. "a beautiful woman" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "a woman of beautiful form [eshet yefat to'ar]" |
| LOGOS | "a beautiful woman" |
| PNEUMA | "a beautiful woman" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *eshet yefat to'ar* = "a woman of beautiful appearance/form." EMET preserves the construct chain with brackets. LOGOS/PNEUMA simplify. Consensus: "a woman of beautiful form" — retaining the distinctive Hebrew phrase as in Gen 29:17 (Rachel) without the bracket (it is self-evident).

---

## v.12 — "pare her nails" vs. "do her nails" vs. "trim her nails"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "she shall do her nails" |
| LOGOS | "she shall pare her nails" |
| PNEUMA | "trim her nails" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *aseta et-tsiporneyha* = "she shall do/make her nails." Exact meaning uncertain. "Pare" (LOGOS) = cut/trim — appropriate and avoids over-specification. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.13 — "weep" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "mourn" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "weep for her father and her mother" |
| LOGOS | "weep for her father and her mother" |
| PNEUMA | "mourn her father and mother" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *u-vakheta* = "and she shall weep." Literal. "Mourn" (PNEUMA) is broader but less precise. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS ("weep").

---

## v.14 — "humiliated" (LOGOS) vs. "humbled" (EMET) vs. "made use of" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "because you have humbled her" |
| LOGOS | "since you have humiliated her" |
| PNEUMA | "because you have made use of her" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ki ineytah* (Pi'el of *anah*) = "because you have humbled/afflicted/violated her." In this coercive context, "humiliated" (LOGOS) carries the appropriate weight. PNEUMA's "made use of" is too euphemistic, erasing the acknowledgment of harm. EMET's "humbled" is accurate but softer. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.15 — "two wives" / relationship description

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the one loved and the other unloved" |
| LOGOS | "the one loved and the other unloved" |
| PNEUMA | "one loved and the other unloved" |

**Ruling:** Minimal variation. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.16 — "causes his sons to inherit" (EMET) vs. "assigns his possessions as an inheritance" (LOGOS) vs. "give his sons their inheritance" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "on the day he causes his sons to inherit what he has" |
| LOGOS | "on the day when he assigns his possessions as an inheritance to his sons" |
| PNEUMA | "When the time comes to give his sons their inheritance" |

**Ruling:** EMET is literal but awkward. LOGOS is the clearest formal rendering. PNEUMA loses "the day" (*yom*) and rephrases completely. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.17 — "first of his vigor" (EMET) vs. "firstfruits of his strength" (LOGOS) vs. "first evidence of his father's strength" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the first of his vigor" |
| LOGOS | "the firstfruits of his strength" |
| PNEUMA | "the first evidence of his father's strength" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *reshit ono* = "beginning/firstfruits of his vigor/strength." EMET: "first of his vigor." LOGOS: "firstfruits of his strength." PNEUMA adds "evidence" and "father's" without textual basis. Consensus follows EMET ("first of his vigor") — compact and literal.

---

## v.19 — "at the gate of his place" (EMET) vs. "at the gate of his city" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to the elders of his city and to the gate of his place" |
| LOGOS | "to the elders of his city at the gate of his city" |
| PNEUMA | "to the elders at the gate of his town" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *sha'ar mekomo* = "the gate of his place." EMET preserves "place" (*maqom*). LOGOS harmonizes to "his city" throughout. "Gate of his city" is a standard formula in Deuteronomy and is contextually accurate. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.21 — "stone him with stones" (EMET) vs. "stone him to death with stones" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "stone him with stones, and he shall die" |
| LOGOS | "stone him to death with stones" |
| PNEUMA | "stone him to death" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *we-saqalu oto be-avanim ve-met* = "and they shall stone him with stones and he shall die." EMET is literal (two clauses). LOGOS compresses death into "to death." PNEUMA drops "with stones." Consensus follows LOGOS ("stone him to death with stones").

---

## v.23 — "curse of God" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "under God's curse" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "a hanged man is a curse of God [qilelat Elohim]" |
| LOGOS | "a hanged man is a curse of God" |
| PNEUMA | "anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *qilelat Elohim taluy* = "a curse of God [is] the hanged one." EMET/LOGOS: "a curse of God" — preserving the genitive ambiguity. PNEUMA: "under God's curse" — resolves toward the passive sense (matching LXX *kekataramenos hypo Theou*). Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS, preserving the MT ambiguity.

---

*SOPHIA — Deuteronomy 21 disagreements logged: 18*
